video_title: Scaling Laws: New Paths to Social Mobility with Joe Seddon
transcript: Joe, welcome to Scaling Laws. >> Great to be on the podcast, Kevin. >> So, I try to maintain a healthy diet of podcasts, you know, my own food pyramid of podcast diversification, which means looking for AI policy solutions, whether it's from the BBC or any podcast here in the States or any podcast really where folks are just throwing big ideas out there and presenting tangible solutions to them because ultimately we just need folks who are doing things to address problems right now. And that's how I found you, Joe. I was stumbling across my podcast feed. I think I was looking for things on workforce development. And all of the sudden I see this podcast with some dude named Joe talking about some organization called Zero Gravity. And I gave it a listen and I don't want to spoil it for the listeners. But it was very compelling and I thought, I've got to have this guy on Scaling Laws. I sent a message on X. Joe, you have an open invite to Scaling Laws and you accepted. So, first and foremost, thank you for, you know, taking a cold DM and saying why the heck not text to the talk to this random guy in Texas. >> There we go. It's It's the power of social media, isn't it? Which is a very pertinent topic in the UK this week cuz the UK government has banned social media for under 16-year-olds. But without Twitter X, I probably wouldn't be here today. So, yeah, really appreciate you reaching out to me and looking forward to have a conversation about how AI is not just going to reshape the face of our education system and career systems, but also the kind of social consequences of what I'm probably sure we're both aligned on is going to be the big technological force of of our lifetimes at least. >> Yeah, and I think in other news that came out this week, we're talking in mid-June for all those folks who want to realize how out of date whatever we're saying is when when this podcast comes out. Earlier this week Anthropic released a study of Claude code usage. And something that stood out to me was a finding that really they're seeing that folks who are getting the most out of Claude aren't the most tech-savvy. They're not the, you know, computer science major. They're not the AI expert. Instead, it's the folks who have the deepest domain expertise for whatever it is that they're actually working on. And this is a major conversation in the workforce development conversation and in the broader pipeline development conversation, which is to say, "Hey, we know that being an expert in any domain has always been lucrative and a sort of goal for any country, for any state, for any workforce entity." And yet a lot of folks are concerned about this missing ladder. You know, the first rung of finding your way into building that expertise maybe moving or shifting as a result of AI, where we find that the demand for that junior engineer or that junior lawyer, so on and so forth. That demand is decreasing. But if we know that domain expertise is the thing that is somewhat AI resistant right now, we've got to find a way to help people develop those skills, get that taste in how to use AI, and make sure that they're adding to whatever their domain is. And so, that sets up sets us up nicely to go back to basics and just help introduce Zero Gravity to folks across the pond here in the US who have never heard of your organization. What is it and why did you start it? >> Sure. So, my journey started in a small town in the north of the UK called Morley, which is a sort of suburban post-industrial market town, not very much opportunity there. And I was born in single-parent family. My mom works as a speech therapist in the National Health Service in the UK, which is the sort of state-run healthcare system. And I just saw firsthand, you know, growing up in that kind of environment, how difficult it was for young people like me to defy the odds and break into elite institutions. And when I say elite institutions, I don't just mean these incredible, world-renowned, you know, universities and colleges like, you know, Oxford, Cambridge, Harvard, Stanford, but but also elite jobs as well. The sort of jobs that you see on on TV but feel completely out of reach for a lot of people living outside urban hubs, not just in the UK but across the developed world. But I was one of the lucky ones. I I managed to make a journey through state-funded education to Oxford University, which was a a life-transforming moment for someone like me. But I was very much an outlier. Uh in the year that I graduated from Oxford, not a single person from the parliamentary constituency I grew up with got a place. And there's literally 100,000 people in these parliamentary constituencies. So, just does show the kind of level and depth of disadvantage where if you do grow up in a small town, a suburb, and you're not from a kind of moneyed family, getting into elite institution is very, very tough. And I set out to change that. And that's where Zero Gravity came from. >> I think it's it's such a great mission, and it's important to to highlight, I think, for US listeners, if you if you can, describe just how disparate access to opportunity is in the UK based off of geography. Because I think that here in the States, obviously, we know that some states, some rural communities in particular, probably have similar statistics in terms of, you know, you you may be one in 100,000 uh or one in a million to find your way to certain institutions. But I feel like the the barriers to entry are particularly pronounced from a geographic standpoint in the UK. Is is that the case and why do you think that is? Why is it so hard for someone from Morley to find their way to to Cambridge or Oxford? >> Yes, so this problem that I'm describing, which is is often labeled by academics as as social mobility, which is just a abstract word for the idea of how much does your background dictate your lot in life, is is an issue not just in the UK but across the across the globe. And and one way to kind of look at this and measure it is Goldman Sachs actually did a interesting piece of research a couple of years ago where they came up with a measure of social mobility, which they called the intergenerational elasticity of earnings, which essentially looks at how much can you predict someone's earnings from how much their parents made growing up. And in the UK, you can essentially across the population predict around 45% of people's earnings based on knowing what their parents earned uh when they grew up. And and the US is actually higher than the UK. So that in the US there is kind of more of a intergenerational stickiness of of income, which I suppose is somewhat surprising cuz that idea of social mobility in many ways is infused right into the core of the American value system, I suppose, of ideas around the American dream, which is probably a more kind of concrete emotional way to talk about the same issue. But it's it's not just the UK that's got a problem in this regard, it's it's every developed economy. Uh but some countries do much better than than others. So we look when you look at some of the Western European counterparts to the UK like uh the Netherlands for instance, you can predict only around 30% of someone's earnings. So some countries do this better than others, but certainly in the Anglosphere countries, uh the UK and the US no don't do a particularly good job in terms of creating that mobile society where your background isn't a massive predictor of what you can can achieve. And I suppose making that more human, I saw it first hand growing up. You know, living in a small town, there isn't lots of people in professional jobs, there isn't lots of opportunities, you don't have the network. State-funded schooling is not always the best. You know, people don't have the financial firepower to just kind of oil the machine of opportunity in life, whether it's getting tutors or textbooks or extra curriculars. And all of these things kind of compound across your lifespan that to create a reality where when you look at who actually gets into these elite institutions, it's predominantly dominated by people who come from affluent backgrounds. If you look at the demography of people who go to Oxford and Cambridge University in the UK or the Ivy League colleges in the US, the the the biggest marker is is not gender, it's not even race, it's socio-economic background. If you wanted one thing to predict who goes to a good college or university versus who doesn't go at all, socio-economic background would be your best predictor of that. So, it's it's a global problem, but the US and UK don't do a good job. And I just believe, I suppose cuz I've got skin in the game in this, having come from one of those backgrounds, that getting this right is existential. And I believe it's not an intractable problem, it's a solvable problem, which is why I set out to try and change it with Zero Gravity. >> Well, and before we get into the mechanics of Zero Gravity, just to highlight your story a little bit further, what was it for you that said, "Hey, I can be that one in 100,000 person. I'm going to be the one who, despite having increased friction relative to, you know, someone from a relatively similar background, let's say, in London, I'm going to defy the odds. I'm going to find the way through these machinery complicated processes and be the one who makes it." What was it that inspired you and drove you to persevere through all of that? >> Well, I think there was a few things. I think number one is that parents and family members are incredibly important. Even if you don't come from an affluent background, having a parental figure who sort of creates a education culture in your household or kind of has the inside track on what's going on is really important. So, I grew up in a single parent background and I had various forms of adversity that I had to overcome growing up, but my mom now instilled very strongly within me a set of values around now valuing education, working hard, being conscientious, which I suppose set me up for success and gave me that bedrock and foundation. So, that was important and a necessary condition, but not not a sufficient one. I think the other thing that was really important actually was the advent of the sort of digital ecosystem around now social media and sort of media consumption platforms. I remember now growing up in my teens with now Facebook and Instagram and YouTube. And even though there's plenty of downsides these platforms, what they did open up was a kind of gateway into another world, a world that I couldn't necessarily see out some of my bedroom window. I was able to like look at YouTube videos of people who attended these incredible universities or had these incredible careers and it opened my eyes to a degree where even if these people weren't always relatable figures, I could at least get a sense of what was out there. So, I think without platforms like now YouTube for instance, the idea of even going to Oxford University or having any sense of what that looked like would have seemed completely alien to me. So, I think we we shouldn't underestimate how much the web has democratized access to information and inspiration. However, I think there's a difference between being inspired and having sort of lots of cold information versus genuinely having the kind of support and inside track to turn information into a plan for action. And I think that's what's been missing until recently. However, excitingly, I think if AI is built with real intentionality, There is potential to maybe they create systems that allow people from backgrounds without networks, without money, uh without connections to navigate these systems in a better better way, which is why I think this technology could potentially be a force for good in some areas if it's applied in the right way. >> Yeah, and it's amazing how it's both a very human element of saying, "Hey, having a mom who cares about education." Or uh having that mentor in your life who says, "Hey Joe, you're capable of great things. Go and try this. Go and do this. You are, you know, uh able to kind of climb those ranks and find your way into these institutions." Combined with the digital possibilities of just exposing you to different universities that you would never have seen and showing you the pathways that you may not have known of, I feel like the conversation is usually not accepting of that nuance, right? We'd prefer we just you know, folks either say, "Hey, it's got to be human only or let's get rid of these apps." As you noted earlier, let's put significant safeguards around these. Versus as you're calling for, and I think it's really healthy, how do we maximize and pair both the human element of making sure that you have that support system with how do we leverage these incredible tools that can show you whole new futures that you didn't realize? I mean, one of the programs that I know is big here in the states is just a college visit program, right? More and more schools are making sure and more and more organizations are making sure that at some point you just go visit a college campus when you are in high school or middle school. Why? So, you just go and see it. You see that it's a cool place to be. You you get a sense of the vibes. You see that it may be somewhere that you want to go and where you aspire to end up. But why not, to your point Joe? I mean, obviously I want everyone to go and visit as many college campuses as they'd like. Everyone's welcome here in Austin. I will buy them breakfast tacos. Um why not have VR systems, right, that make that experience a virtual one and immersive for the person who couldn't make that trip? Or, you know, they want to go visit a different campus or visit in air quotes here. We can leverage these tools to to help with that. And so, with that sort of intervention in mind, what is the particular steps that Zero Gravity is taking to help pair people with that better, brighter future? >> Well, I think that the first step is identification, which is people don't walk around with a sign on their head that says, "I'm from a low-income background or I'm from a background without very much opportunity or or connections." No, this sort of characteristic of, you know, socioeconomic status or class or or income, whatever you want to call it, is is in some ways intangible or or is more intangible with other characteristics. So, a lot of people from disadvantaged backgrounds A don't know they're disadvantaged to begin with, even if especially if they live in the place where other people are like them, which tends to be more the case of people living in suburban and rural areas versus cities where you have more kind of vivid inequality. So, people don't know they're disadvantaged, and then people also often don't know that they've got what it takes to defy the odds. So, what we've done is we've developed an algorithm that we've connected into around one in three of all the state-funded schools in the UK that every year pinpoints the students in those schools who are from these sort of disadvantaged backgrounds, but we believe have the potential to break into top universities and careers. And we aggregate lots of different types of data to do that, but essentially we're trying to find these diamonds in the rough who are currently sort of being missed by the conventional system, but we believe with just with a bit of support, a bit of a push, with someone who believes in them can kind of defy the odds and land that future they never thought possible. So, the first step is actually just identifying the talent. The next step is then how do you actually get it in? We're working with people with a lot of raw talent and a lot of raw potential, but it is raw. It's It's talent that needs to be cultivated and developed. So, we tend to work with students from between the 12 to 24 months, and we essentially take them on a journey of compounding where we say, "Look, we're not going to change your odds overnight. We're going to make you 1% better every week over a 1 to 2-year period." And the end result of that is we're going to take you on a journey where you're able to get into one of these elite universities, and we provide all the support and I throw our app. It's available iPhone, Android, and people do a variety of things, whether it's connecting with mentors, attending master classes with top universities and employers, getting exclusive opportunities like those college campus visits you spoke about or an internship with an employer, and connecting with other people as well. Connecting with other people like them because it's easy to underestimate if you're somebody who lives in a disadvantaged community where there's not much opportunity, often you don't have a peer group where you live who understands your ambition and where you want to go. And certainly not a peer group that has the kind of inside track on what it actually takes to land all of those opportunities. So, just surrounding people with a dif- different peer group, even if it's still in a digital environment, you can kind of create that cultural capital that a lot of students are missing where they're suddenly surrounded by people who think like them, have the same ambitions as them, and understand them, and that just creates that flywheel of everyone kind of pushing each other onwards in a kind of nice, sort of semi-competitive, semi-uh sort of supportive way, creating that beautiful flywheel that leads to great outcomes at the end. >> So, there's a lot to explore with your your approach, and I think it's it's fascinating because just on that cultural aspect I you know, I had had a lot of advantages growing up and was was very fortunate to have a stable upbringing and connections to plenty of folks and plenty of opportunities. But even when I first went to Washington DC, I remember that I stuck out like a sore thumb because I went to Men's Wearhouse with my dad. We got the cheapest suits we could find and I show up for this internship on the hill, which again, very privileged to even have that opportunity. But I show up. I didn't know you had to cut the little like threads out of your pockets on on some suits. You know, my tie was a hand-me-down. It was his like AT&T tie from 30 years ago. I think there was a ketchup stain on it. And then you know, there are these folks from the Acela Corridor. They've got these fitted suits. They've got these fancy shoes. I'm wearing I think Costco Kirkland brand like white socks that I just thought, "Hey, they'll get the job done. What's the what's the big deal here?" And not having a cohort of other Oregonians who suddenly found themselves in DC to go and ask, "Hey, how the heck do I not embarrass myself at this cocktail hour?" That would have been huge for me and would have given me a lot more confidence going into those settings. And so just that as you referred to it, that cultural connection and community building is such an important element there. But before we explore that further, I want to go back to the start, which is this this algorithm because I think that folks will either say, "Oh my gosh, Joe is running this dystopian organization that says we're going to cobble together all of your stats, all of your your test scores, your family income, your zip code, generic stats, whatever, and they're going to predetermine whether you or not you get access to this incredible opportunity. Other folks may say, "Hey, this is great. These people may not have otherwise ever been found through traditional means. I mean, there's only one Joe in your organization's only so big, and as you pointed out, and I think rightfully so, and thankfully, folks don't wear a big hat that says, 'Joe, please select me for zero gravity.' So, what does this algorithm look like, and how do you make sure that it doesn't veer into that sort of dystopian kind of environment that that some folks may fear?" >> Sure. So, the kind of philosophy behind the algorithm is to create the most robust insights into someone's background and level of potential in the least invasive way possible. So, we don't ask for people's parents' tax returns or for them to sort of write some kind of sob story of what they've had to overcome. What we look at is number of different data points. So, for instance, if you have someone's home postcode in the UK, you can work out lots of things about the person's background. So, there's data at street level on socioeconomic status, there's data on crime rates in the local area, there's data on how many people in the local area progress to university and into professional jobs. So, just from one piece of data, you can unlock myriad other pieces of data. And we also look at people's schooling as well. So, we look at how good their schools are, how much progress the students make at that school. And we also benchmark people's academic accomplishments against their peer group at school. So, if you go to a school where most people are failing, but you're actually doing pretty well, then that's a great great sign because you might be performing two standard deviations above the mean. However, on a traditional uh resume, you might not be able to capture that because people never heard of your school, and maybe you look rubbish compared to someone who went to a really a school where everyone did really well, but in reality all that top sort of outperformer, but it's just not been uncovered. So, that's the kind of philosophy uh behind what we do and and how we do it. And in terms of your pushback in terms of how do you make sure this doesn't become dystopian, my kind of response that would be I think the way that a lot of institutions have done things historically is far more dystopian than than what we're doing because either you've got institutions who don't use any of these techniques at all, and those institutions are kind of accidentally or unconsciously biasing themselves towards people who had lots of resources growing up. And if you just look at a CV or just look at a resume, then you are going to naturally skew towards people who are able to sort of game the education system through through resources. So, that's already a pretty dystopian world. Or you have institutions who try to do this stuff, but do it really badly. Uh so, I think a a lot of the kind of um programs that have been designed sometimes around race and ethnicity, I think have have sometimes been quite crude in terms of their implementation, where just sort of giving people an opportunity solely based on on skin color, but not taking into account socioeconomic status can can maybe sound you know good on paper in some ways, but lead to some pretty um weird outcomes, where you're able to increase the the ethnic uh representation of your institution, but the people who are getting the opportunities tend to be the really affluent people who just so happen to come from a different different racial background. And that that's particularly been a problem in the UK. I know it's a it's a much bigger and more controversial issue in the US, but a lot of UK-based corporates, universities have created programs uh around race in particular to try and drive diversity. And even though they were successful in terms of increasing ethnic diversity, it turned out it was just still recruiting people who happen to come from higher socioeconomic backgrounds. So, in terms of genuinely representing the country and making sure we're finding talent from all backgrounds, those programs again led to outcomes that are in some in some ways dystopian. Whereas, I suppose if you're a white working-class person living in a small town, those programs don't do anything to uncover your talent. So, I I'd say that that would be my pushback. There's no perfect way to do this, but it's better to focus on the characteristic that really really matters, and that in my opinion is socioeconomic status, and that's the best way to find talent rather focusing on other characteristics. >> And I I like your focus, too, on the sort of compared to what analysis. I always borrow this from Ethan Mollick, but really there's this temptation, as I raised in my initial pushback, of always saying, "Oh, well, it's AI and the use of an algorithm. How dystopian, how scary, so on and so forth." And yet, to your point, Joe, we're not doing a great job under the status quo. And it's amazing. I have a friend who works at a university, and it's her job to go to communities for whom they have lower rates of general acceptance at this university and just say, "Hey, this university exists. We're interested in you applying, and here's how to do it, and we're going to help you through this process." Well, that's fine and dandy, but again, there's only one of her. And whoever is fortunate to be randomly selected by that university as a target community, you know, that's that's to your point, Joe, even more dystopian of you just somebody rolled a die and said, "Hey, we're going to go to this county, but not that county." And if you happen to be in that other non-selected county, well, tough luck, kid. You know, let's let's hope you stumble upon that opportunity down the road. So, let's talk size now. How long have y'all been around? How many folks have gone through the Zero Gravity pipeline? And what what's the sort of effectiveness looking like in terms of helping people identify talent and connect them to opportunities. >> Sure, so it's been a real startup journey. So, I I started Zero Gravity uh straight out of my bedroom when I graduated from university back in 2018. And the first cohort of students on the platform was only around 150 in size. So, I literally knew all the students by name. Uh I I I felt like I was sort of uh vicariously living through their success cuz I knew all of them. And in that first cohort, we managed to get 50 students into Oxford and Cambridge University in the UK, which was no incredible not just in terms of the success rate, but in terms of just the incredible stories that came out of that. in towns in the UK no one had heard of, breaking into probably one of the most illustrious institutions in the world. It was It was quite mind-bending. Um and and since then we've we've been scaling uh to the point now that we've supported over 23,000 students from low-income backgrounds to access leading universities in the UK. So, that that first cohort of 150 is uh multiplied by a multiple orders of of magnitude, which is has been really exciting. Um but we're not stopping here. The the big thing we've been focused on for the past couple of years has been once students get into a great university, how do we help them land that incredible first job? Uh so, we've partnered with a lot of leading employers uh based in the UK to to do that and to break down barriers to their graduate schemes internships. So, we work with the companies that you would have heard of in the states like you know, McKinsey, uh Rolls-Royce, Snapchat, um the pharmaceuticals companies like Roche. So, like a real like mixture of companies, but all with the same goal of how do we make it possible for top companies to find top talent from all backgrounds, not just the talent that happens to kind of land in their their resume uh drawer. >> Well, and I think too, and I wonder if this has been true of your our is there a sense that the folks who've gone through Zero Gravity have that sort of high agency and high rate of perseverance and as everyone's favorite buzzword was for a while there, a high rate of grit, right? Of a of a willingness to just get the job done, stay after hours, you know, go through, talk with that mentor, find them, work with them because they've had this experience with Zero Gravity and they've navigated the system as an underdog that perhaps they're even better than the person who maybe had, you know, a 0.1% higher GPA or something like that. >> Yeah, exactly. Like if you were a a university or an employer and you're recruiting someone into your undergraduate or your your graduate program, like what are you optimizing for at the end of the day? Well, you're optimizing for potential, not absolute performance because you're recruiting a young person who by definition is unproven and you're also optimizing, I think, for resilience. You know, somebody who can, you know, adapt to new environments and they take on a bigger workload, doing stuff they haven't been exposed to before, but still be able to deliver deliver results. And when it comes to optimizing for those two things, I mean, there's no better place to look than these diamonds in the rough because by definition, they're all massive overperformers. They're people who are performing, you know, two standard deviations above what you'd expect for someone in their school or from their background, but they're also hyper-resilient as well because they've had to overcome the adversity of life. That their growing up in a lower socioeconomic background is almost like an arena for building resilience. Like every day you're having to overcome some level of adversity that would just seem completely alien to kids from more affluent backgrounds. So, if you're optimizing for raw talent and optimizing for resilience, there's no better place to look for these than these talented people from lower income backgrounds, but the trade-off is that they're less polished because they haven't had the resource. They're they're a bit like you said, they're they're that kid who turns up uh for the first week of the internship. Maybe they don't have the perfect tie. Maybe they don't have the shiny shoes. Maybe their accent isn't the sort of received pronunciation accent that everyone loves in the UK. And that means that some people might think on paper these people they look too tricky to work with, that they're not able to get results. But it doesn't take long once you actually expose these people to opportunity to get them into a place where they can not only deliver results, but still hold on to the part of themselves that made them unique and interesting in the first place. And that doesn't happen overnight, but I've seen time and time and again these people dramatically surprise you in terms of what they can achieve when you throw them on the off the deep end into an elite institution. >> Yeah, and and it speaks to the fact, too, that we can begin to imagine a world in which increased use of AI in terms of identifying potential, as you noted, and in terms of identifying capacity to apply knowledge to diverse contexts and to to uh dive into new tasks and to be that resilient worker. AI may allow for that sort of identification of those individuals. And identification of individuals as a part of the labor market who previously may have been ignored, right? Because of, as you pointed out, Joe, maybe their accent uh just and just a friendly reminder for our US listeners, your accent is a big deal in the UK. Uh and I I I know you can talk to that more, Joe, if you'd like, but you know, under a traditional interview context, the second you walk into a room and let's say you don't have the right accent, game over, right? Uh you know, that it was a good try. You may have had the best resume, but the hiring manager just doesn't like folks with that accent and you're you're done. But if you have a platform that facilitates labor matching, uh whether it's, you know, worker to employee or let's say student matching when it's a student to a university, perhaps now folks have a greater chance of finding their their way into the door rather than some arbitrary like, "I don't like your accent." >> Yeah, 100% and and that's why I'm I'm bullish on the ability for AI to create far better systems around talent matching, university and career navigation, and indeed just kind of educating people and building up the knowledge than what the conventional sort of 20th century education career system has been able to to achieve. Like, people shouldn't forget that the system that we have now around how people are educated, how the higher education system functions, and how you will get a job hasn't really changed fundamentally in around the 50 years. Like, yes, recruitment systems are digital now, but they're completely manual and in person, but the fundamental structure of how people get these opportunities is is remarkably similar to how it was in the 1980s. And and that is I think one of the reasons we haven't really been able to move the needle in the Western world when it comes to social mobility for for well over a generation now. So, I'm I'm bullish in that regard. I think where I'm more concerned is just regarding some of the more macroeconomic effects of AI that we may see in the medium term, which is just looking at some of the data in the UK across the kind of big enterprise companies that we work with, there's been two consecutive years now of a reduction in the amount of entry-level jobs roughly around the rate of 8% reduction year-on-year. And that means that getting one of these graduate schemes is more competitive than ever before. In the UK on average, there's 140 applications for every graduate role. So, like, you've got less than a 1% chance of success, which is is far lower than any university or college uh application success rate. So, getting a graduate job is far more difficult, but even getting into university. But also as well, I think like looking at the direction of travel, there is a clear scenario, which I don't think is unrealistic, in which this trend continues, and and we sort of see a world where actually there's a shrinking pie of entry-level opportunities, even though there's the same amount of people coming out of the education system. And I mean, that's a dangerous world to live in, because that's a world in which that ladder does start to break down. A world in which you've got students coming out of university still with very high amounts of student debt, still with very high amounts of ambition, but ambition which isn't able to easily be realized in terms of stepping straight into one of those graduate roles. And I think that's going to be a big issue for policy makers and companies to grapple with, I think, over the next couple of years. Which is even if we build these AI systems help people navigate, if those entry-level opportunities aren't there, that's going to create a very Darwinian world in terms of the winners and losers of this of this system. >> Well, in two we know that having a sense of meaning and having a sense of purpose is important for everyone of of any age. But to have that initial passion and excitement of oh, I finally get to enter the real world and apply all these skills to meaningful problems, so on and so forth. To have that stymied right out of graduation is, to your point Joe, not a recipe for success and not a recipe for a political community that's going to be trusting, that's going to support existing institutions, that's going to go volunteer in their community, and so on and so forth. Because there's that perception, real or perceived, that they've been wronged or that they've had, you know, that ladder as we talked about earlier, the first rung got cut. It was promised, but it's not there anymore. So, what gives? Uh and I I think Joe, I'm going to ask you to you know, wear a little bit of a policy hat given that you're talking with Fortune 500 companies here in the states. You're talking with leading educational institutions in the UK and here in the states as well. Where do we go from here? How do we help bridge that gap between hey, you've done a great job in school. You you've worked your butt off. Sorry, we're not sure where you go from here.
